coordinates.description: Seattle reference; breeds in dry woodland habitats of western North America from southern British Columbia through California. In the PNW, found in Garry oak woodlands of the south Puget Sound lowlands, San Juan Islands, Willamette Valley, and in ponderosa pine/oak habitats east of the Cascades. The coordinates mark the broader PNW range; primary habitat is the dry woodland belt where Garry oaks and ponderosa pines share open, sun-dappled canopy.

taxonomy_note.description: Black-throated Gray Warbler is the SIXTH Parulidae in the engine, returning after an 18-degree absence (the longest family gap followed by return). Last Parulidae was Orange-crowned Warbler at degree 20. The warbler quintet (14-17, 20) was structurally closed -- five song endings, five genera. This sixth member opens a new chapter: different energy (E=4 vs. quintet's E=3), different ecosystem (dry woodland vs. wet/mixed), different timbre (buzzy vs. clean/thin), different tempo (slow vs. moderate/fast). Second Setophaga in the engine (after Yellow Warbler at degree 16).
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Setophaga = Ancient Greek ses/setos (moth) + phagos (eater) = 'moth-eater.' Nigrescens = Latin present participle of nigrescere = 'to become black' or 'to darken.' Full translation: 'the darkening moth-eater' -- an insectivore whose plumage is in the process of becoming black. The species epithet describes a becoming, not a being. Formerly Dendroica nigrescens before the 2011 AOS genus merger [NEEDS VERIFICATION -- approximate date].
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Setophaga is the largest New World warbler genus with approximately 34 species [NEEDS VERIFICATION]. This is the engine's second Setophaga (after Yellow Warbler, S. petechia, at degree 16). The genus pair creates a maximum contrast: Yellow Warbler = brightest, most colorful warbler in the engine; Black-throated Gray = most graphic, least colorful (black-and-white with one yellow dot). Same genus, opposite aesthetics.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Passeriformes returns after three consecutive Apodiformes (degrees 35-37, the hummingbird trio). The engine spent three degrees outside Passeriformes -- the longest non-Passeriformes streak. Degree 38 breaks the streak and returns to the songbird order.
taxonomy_note.warbler_quintet_note: The warbler quintet (degrees 14-17, 20) mapped five song endings: resolution (14), interruption (15), climax (16), repetition (17), evaporation (20). The quintet is structurally closed. The Black-throated Gray Warbler does not extend the quintet -- it opens a new Parulidae expansion beyond the quintet's structural completeness.
taxonomy_note.dendroica_note: Formerly placed in genus Dendroica (Greek dendron = tree + oikos = dwelling = 'tree-dweller'). The 2011 AOS merger of Dendroica into Setophaga was based on molecular phylogenetics showing that the American Redstart (original Setophaga) was nested within Dendroica, requiring priority rules to merge the larger genus into the older name [NEEDS VERIFICATION]. Most field guides published before 2012 list this species as Dendroica nigrescens.
taxonomy_note.family_lead: With 6 members, Parulidae now LEADS all families in the engine, surpassing Hirundinidae and Turdidae (5 each). This is appropriate -- Parulidae is the largest songbird family in North America (~115 species). Six of 115 is barely 5%.

song_metrics.note_structure: Series of 4-5 buzzy, wheezy phrases: weazy weazy weazy wee-zy. Each phrase is a brief buzzy note with rough timbre. The first 3-4 phrases are typically at the same pitch (isometric). The final phrase often rises or drops slightly, creating a gentle terminal accent.
song_metrics.note_types: TWO sound categories: (1) PRIMARY SONG -- 4-5 buzzy weazy phrases, slow tempo, lazy delivery. The signature sound. (2) CALL NOTE -- a flat, dull tup or tchep, used as contact and alarm call. Less sharp than most warbler chip notes.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Isometric initial phrases (same pitch, same duration) followed by a terminal phrase with slight pitch change. The overall contour is FLAT then ACCENTED -- minimal change across the song with a subtle ending gesture. This is the least dramatic song architecture of any warbler in the engine.
song_metrics.timbre: BUZZY -- the defining quality. Rough, husky, insect-like. More buzz than whistle. Dramatically different from the clean pure tones of Yellow Warbler (16) or the thin trill of Orange-crowned (20). The buzziest warbler song in the engine.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4 -- FIRST Parulidae above E=3. All five quintet members were E=3. The Black-throated Gray breaks the pattern. The song is moderate in volume -- carries adequately through open oak canopy but is not a loud singer compared to Yellowthroat (17) or Yellow Warbler (16).
song_metrics.tempo: SLOW for a warbler -- approximately 2-3 phrases per second [NEEDS VERIFICATION]. Noticeably slower than Orange-crowned trill (8-16 notes/s) or Wilson's accelerando. This is the laziest tempo in the engine's Parulidae.
song_metrics.context: Males sing from exposed perches in the oak/pine canopy during breeding season (May-July). Song is territorial and mate-attraction. Less persistent than Yellowthroat -- does not sing all day. Morning chorus peak.
song_metrics.key_property: THE DRAWL -- the 39th acoustic element. A slow, buzzy, unhurried warbler song delivered with the relaxed confidence of a bird that is exactly where it wants to be. After three hummingbird sounds (thrum, sonic boom, filament -- all mechanical or threadlike), the engine shifts to a vocal drawl: slow tempo, buzzy timbre, lazy delivery. The word 'drawl' captures the unhurried, elongated quality of each phrase -- like a slow Southern accent in birdsong. The drawl is the opposite of urgency (15). It is the most relaxed sound in the Parulidae.

plumage.overall: Black, white, and gray -- a bird drawn in ink on white paper. No color except one small yellow loral spot between eye and bill. Bold black-and-white facial pattern, gray back with black streaks, white underparts with black flank streaks, two white wing bars.
plumage.head: Bold geometric pattern: black crown, white supercilium (eyebrow stripe), black cheek patch, white malar stripe. The face is a field mark -- high-contrast black-and-white stripes.
plumage.throat: BLACK in males (diagnostic). Gray in females and immatures.
plumage.yellow_spot: Small but diagnostic patch of YELLOW between eye and bill (loral spot). The single point of warm color on an otherwise achromatic bird. Visible in the field. Separates Black-throated Gray from similar black-and-white warblers. The minimum amount of color that functions as a field mark.
plumage.body: White underparts with black streaks on flanks. Gray back with black streaks. Two white wing bars. White outer tail feathers.
plumage.field_id: Black-and-white face pattern with black throat (male). Gray back. White wing bars. One small yellow dot between eye and bill. Overall impression: ink drawing with one dot of color. The graphic warbler.
plumage.setophaga_contrast: Yellow Warbler (degree 16, S. petechia) = entirely vivid yellow. Black-throated Gray Warbler (degree 38, S. nigrescens) = entirely achromatic except one yellow dot. Same genus, maximum aesthetic contrast. The highlighter pen and the ink drawing.

oak_pine_ecology.definition: Oak-pine -- dry woodland ecosystem characterized by Garry oak (Quercus garryana), ponderosa pine (Pinus ponderosa), or both in open, savanna-like arrangements. Sparse understory of bunchgrass and wildflowers. Sun-dappled canopy. Low to moderate elevation (0-1,500 m). Rain shadow or dry lowland habitat. The engine's FIRST dry woodland ecosystem.

garry_oak.species: Quercus garryana -- the PNW's ONLY native oak species
garry_oak.range: Southern British Columbia through Washington and Oregon to northern California. Dry lowland areas with moderate precipitation.
garry_oak.conservation: Among the most endangered ecosystems in the PNW. Less than 10% of historical extent remains intact. Critically imperiled (S1) in British Columbia, imperiled (S2) in Washington [NEEDS VERIFICATION].
garry_oak.threats: Fire suppression (Douglas fir encroachment), urban development, agricultural conversion, invasive species (Scotch broom, Himalayan blackberry).

pnw_distribution: South Puget Sound lowlands -- Joint Base Lewis-McChord area, Mima Mounds, Scatter Creek
San Juan Islands -- dryland oak forests
Willamette Valley -- remaining oak prairie and woodland patches
Eastern Cascades foothills -- ponderosa pine/oak transition
Rogue Valley, southern Oregon -- oak-pine woodland
oak_pine_ecology.significance: FIRST DRY WOODLAND in the engine. All 37 previous degrees used wet, urban, montane, or generalized ecosystems. Oak-pine introduces the dry side of the PNW -- the rain shadow, the oak savanna, the sunny woodland that most visitors never see. The PNW is not just rain and conifers. It is also oaks and pines and bunchgrass and sun.

threats: Garry oak habitat loss from fire suppression and Douglas fir encroachment
Urban development in south Puget Sound and Willamette Valley
conservation.habitat_paradox: The bird is secure (Least Concern) but its primary PNW habitat (Garry oak woodland) is critically endangered. The species persists because it uses multiple dry woodland types across its range, not just Garry oak. But PNW Garry oak populations are declining as habitat disappears.
conservation.indicator_species: Where the Black-throated Gray Warbler sings, the oaks are still standing. The species serves as an indicator of oak woodland health in the PNW.

theory_nodes.concept: The drawl -- the 39th acoustic element: a slow, buzzy, unhurried warbler song delivered from an oak-pine canopy, each phrase given its full measure of time, no acceleration, no crescendo, no evaporation. After three hummingbird sounds (thrum, sonic boom, filament) that were all about extremes -- speed, explosion, minimum mass -- the drawl decelerates the engine into warbler time. The tempo shifts from milliseconds (hummingbird dive) to full seconds (warbler phrase). The timbre shifts from mechanical/threadlike to vocal/buzzy. The drawl is the sound of a bird that is exactly where it wants to be and is in no hurry to be anywhere else.
examples: Black-throated Gray Warbler song: weazy weazy weazy wee-zy. 4-5 buzzy phrases at ~2-3 per second [NEEDS VERIFICATION]. The slowest warbler tempo in the engine.

The hummingbird-to-warbler transition: thrum (35, continuous mechanical) -> sonic boom (36, explosive mechanical) -> filament (37, threadlike vocal whisper) -> drawl (38, slow buzzy vocal song). Three degrees of hummingbird extremes followed by one degree of warbler relaxation.

A drawl in human speech: slow, relaxed pronunciation where vowels stretch and consonants soften. The Black-throated Gray's song drawls -- each buzzy phrase gets its full duration, unhurried, unpressing.

Connection to buzz (degree 32, Bohemian Waxwing): both are buzzy sounds, but the Bohemian buzz was collective and assertive (E=5, hundreds of birds). The drawl is solitary and lazy (E=4, one bird in an oak). Social buzz vs. solitary drawl.

The drawl is the antithesis of urgency (degree 15, Wilson's Warbler). Wilson's accelerated. The drawl does not accelerate. It sits. It repeats. It does not hurry. The engine needed a warbler that was content to be slow.
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: If the Wilson's Warbler (degree 15) is a presto passage -- notes getting faster, building to a cutoff -- then the Black-throated Gray Warbler is a largo: measured, deliberate, each note given its full rhythmic value. In orchestral terms, the drawl is a bassoon melody played at walking tempo, slightly buzzy in timbre, repeating a simple phrase with minor variation at the end. Not flashy. Not dramatic. The movement that holds the suite together by refusing to rush.

theory_nodes.concept: Parulidae returns after 18 degrees -- the longest family gap followed by a return in the engine. The warbler quintet (14-17, 20) closed with the Orange-crowned Warbler's evaporation. The engine then explored eight other families across eighteen degrees. The Black-throated Gray Warbler reopens Parulidae with a fundamentally different character: different energy (E=4 vs. E=3), different ecosystem (dry woodland vs. wet), different timbre (buzzy vs. clean), different tempo (slow vs. fast). The return is not a continuation. It is a restatement in a new key.
examples: The 18-degree gap: degrees 21-37 explored Picidae, Cardinalidae, Turdidae, Hirundinidae, Bombycillidae, Fringillidae, Trochilidae. Eight families in the absence of warblers.
Quintet vs. sixth member: the quintet was all E=3, wet-side, clean-toned, moderate-to-fast tempo. The sixth is E=4, dry-side, buzzy, slow. The break is clean.
Family lead: Parulidae now has 6 members, surpassing Hirundinidae and Turdidae (5 each). First family to reach 6 degrees.
Setophaga recurrence: Yellow Warbler (16) and Black-throated Gray (38) are both Setophaga. 22-degree gap within the genus. Maximum aesthetic contrast within the same genus.
The return signal: Parulidae's reappearance after 18 degrees signals that the engine cycles back to families. No family is done after its first arc. The quintet was chapter one. Chapter two begins.
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: In sonata form, the recapitulation restates the exposition's themes in the home key -- but transformed by the development section. The Parulidae return at degree 38 is a recapitulation: the warbler theme returns, but transformed. The key has changed (E=3 to E=4), the instrumentation has changed (clean to buzzy), the tempo has changed (fast to slow), the setting has changed (wet to dry). It is recognizably Parulidae. But it is not the quintet. It is the warbler theme after the engine has learned from eighteen degrees of other families.

theory_nodes.concept: Oak-pine ecosystem -- the engine's first dry woodland habitat. A fundamentally new biome that introduces the rain shadow side of the PNW: Garry oak savanna, ponderosa pine forest, sun-dappled canopy, bunchgrass understory. After 37 degrees of wet, urban, montane, and aerial ecosystems, the engine finally touches dry woodland. The PNW is not just rain and conifers -- it is also oaks and pines and sun. The Garry oak is the PNW's only native oak, and its ecosystem is among the most endangered in the region.
examples: Garry oak (Quercus garryana): PNW's only native oak. Less than 10% of historical range intact [NEEDS VERIFICATION]. Fire suppression allows Douglas fir to shade out oaks.
Ecosystem contrast: all 37 previous degrees used wet, urban, montane, or generalized habitats. Oak-pine is the first explicitly DRY habitat. The engine adds a new climate axis.
PNW Garry oak locations: south Puget Sound, San Juan Islands, Willamette Valley -- not the places tourists photograph, but critical habitat.

Connection to mountain conifer (degree 33) and cone crop (degree 34): ponderosa pine appears in all three. But mountain conifer is high elevation and cone crop is irruptive. Oak-pine is low elevation and stable.
Endangered ecosystem with a secure species: the bird is Least Concern but its best PNW habitat is critically imperiled. The conservation paradox: a common bird in a rare habitat.
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: The key change from minor to major. The engine has been in the wet, dense, minor-key PNW for 37 degrees. The oak-pine woodland is the relative major: open, sunny, warm, dry. Same root (Pacific Northwest), different mode. The drawl -- lazy, buzzy, unhurried -- is a song that could only exist in this major-key landscape. The wet-side warblers sang in minor. The dry-side warbler drawls in major.

theory_nodes.concept: The Setophaga pair -- Yellow Warbler (degree 16, S. petechia) and Black-throated Gray Warbler (degree 38, S. nigrescens). Same genus, maximum contrast. The Yellow Warbler is the brightest warbler in the engine -- vivid yellow, ascending crescendo, E=3, riparian willow. The Black-throated Gray is the most graphic -- black and white with one yellow dot, lazy buzzy drawl, E=4, oak-pine. The genus Setophaga ('moth-eater') now spans from maximum color to minimum color, from wet riparian to dry woodland, from ascending energy to steady-state buzz. One genus contains both the highlighter pen and the ink drawing.
examples: Plumage contrast: Yellow Warbler = entirely vivid yellow with chestnut streaks. Black-throated Gray = entirely achromatic (black, white, gray) with one small yellow loral spot. Same genus, opposite color palettes.

Song contrast: Yellow = sweet-sweet-sweeter, ascending crescendo, clean tones. Black-throated Gray = weazy weazy weazy wee-zy, steady-state drawl, buzzy tones. Same genus, opposite acoustic strategies.
Ecosystem contrast: Yellow = riparian willow (wet). Black-throated Gray = oak-pine (dry). Same genus, opposite moisture regimes.
Energy contrast: Yellow = E=3 (moderate-active). Black-throated Gray = E=4 (moderate-steady). Same genus, different energy character (one builds, one maintains).
The yellow dot: the Black-throated Gray's one loral spot of yellow is the genus signature -- a trace of the Yellow Warbler's saturated yellow, reduced to a single point. The minimum Setophaga.
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Two movements of the same concerto played in different modes. The Yellow Warbler is the major-key allegro -- bright, ascending, vivid. The Black-throated Gray is the minor-key andante -- monochrome, steady, restrained. Same thematic material (Setophaga), same structural function (Parulidae), completely different character. The concerto needs both movements.

theory_nodes.concept: The yellow loral spot -- one point of warm color on an achromatic bird. The minimum field mark. Connects to the hidden crown of the Orange-crowned Warbler (degree 20): two warblers, two strategies of minimal color. The Orange-crowned hides its color (concealed orange crown, named 'celata' = hidden). The Black-throated Gray shrinks its color to a single visible dot. Both challenge the assumption that a field mark should be conspicuous. Both are Parulidae. Both teach that identity can be found in what is barely there.
examples: Orange-crowned Warbler (degree 20): orange crown patch concealed beneath olive feathers. Named for a feature almost never visible. celata = 'hidden.'
Black-throated Gray Warbler (degree 38): yellow loral spot visible but tiny. One dot of color on a monochrome bird. nigrescens = 'darkening.'
Yellow Warbler (degree 16): entirely vivid yellow. The MAXIMUM color warbler. The Black-throated Gray's single yellow dot is a trace of what the Yellow Warbler has in abundance.
The spectrum: maximum color (Yellow, degree 16) -> hidden color (Orange-crowned, degree 20) -> minimal visible color (Black-throated Gray, degree 38). Three Parulidae, three color strategies.
Diagnostic function: the yellow loral spot is the feature that separates Black-throated Gray from Black-and-white Warbler and Blackpoll Warbler. One dot of yellow does the work of identification.
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: The grace note -- a single ornamental note that is technically not part of the melody but changes the character of the phrase. The yellow loral spot is a chromatic grace note on an achromatic melody. Without it, the bird is a generic black-and-white warbler. With it, the bird is Setophaga nigrescens. One note. One dot. One point of color that makes identity.

theory_nodes.concept: E=4 continues on species side -- the Black-throated Gray Warbler is the FIRST Parulidae above E=3. All five quintet members (degrees 14-17, 20) were E=3. The sixth warbler breaks the energy ceiling for the family. E=4 now at 15 degrees (38.5% of 39 entries). E=3 still absent for 18 degrees (since degree 20). The engine's dynamic range compression continues -- E=3, E=2, and E=1 are all absent from the recent arc. The upper half of the energy spectrum (E=4 and E=5) dominates the last 18 degrees.
examples: Quintet energy: all E=3 (degrees 14, 15, 16, 17, 20). The warbler quintet occupied the exact middle of the energy range.
Sixth warbler: E=4 -- one step above the quintet's uniform E=3. The dry woodland warbler sings with slightly more moderate energy than the wet-side warblers.
E=4 count: now 15 degrees (38.5%). E=5: 3 degrees (7.7%). E=3: 7 degrees (17.9%) -- but the last E=3 was 18 degrees ago.
Dynamic range compression: degrees 21-38 have been exclusively E=4 and E=5. NO E=3, E=2, or E=1 in 18 degrees. The lower half of the energy spectrum is unrepresented in the recent arc.
The structural need: E=3 or lower. The engine urgently needs middle-register and quiet species to restore dynamic range.
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: An orchestra that has been playing mezzo-forte to fortissimo for eighteen measures. The string section needs to come down to piano. The percussion section needs to rest. The drawl is mf -- not loud, but not quiet either. The engine is stuck in the upper dynamic range. A pianissimo passage is overdue.

cross_references.title: Orange-crowned Warbler (Degree 20)
cross_references.connection: PARULIDAE RETURNS. Eighteen-degree gap -- the longest family absence followed by a return. Orange-crowned = the evaporation ending (descending trill, E=3, shrubby slope). Black-throated Gray = the drawl (lazy buzzy wheeze, E=4, oak-pine). The quintet's closer and the sextet's opener. Two warblers separated by 18 degrees. Orange-crowned faded into silence. Black-throated Gray drawls with lazy confidence. The family's tone has changed.

cross_references.title: Yellow Warbler (Degree 16)
cross_references.connection: SETOPHAGA PAIR. Same genus, maximum contrast. Yellow Warbler = brightest, most colorful warbler (vivid yellow, ascending crescendo, E=3, riparian willow). Black-throated Gray = most graphic warbler (black-and-white with one yellow dot, lazy drawl, E=4, oak-pine). The genus contains both the highlighter pen and the ink drawing. Twenty-two degrees between Setophaga appearances.

cross_references.title: MacGillivray's Warbler (Degree 14)
cross_references.connection: WARBLER QUINTET FIRST MEMBER. MacGillivray's introduced Parulidae with a narrative song (AAB, terminal resolution, E=3, wet understory). Black-throated Gray reopens Parulidae 24 degrees later with a buzzy drawl (steady-state, E=4, dry canopy). First warbler vs. sixth warbler. Wet skulker vs. dry singer.

cross_references.title: Calliope Hummingbird (Degree 37)
cross_references.connection: HUMMINGBIRD TRIO CLOSES, WARBLER RETURNS. Three consecutive Apodiformes (35-37) end. Passeriformes returns. From the smallest bird in North America (3.1 g, filament) to a normal-sized warbler (~8 g, drawl). From mountain meadow to oak-pine. From threadlike to buzzy. The engine shifts from hummingbird extremes to warbler normalcy.

cross_references.title: Bohemian Waxwing (Degree 32)
cross_references.connection: BUZZ CONNECTION. Two buzzy sounds. Bohemian Waxwing = the buzz (E=5, collective bzeee, assertive, hundreds of birds). Black-throated Gray = the drawl (E=4, solo weazy, lazy, one bird). Social buzz vs. solitary drawl. The engine's two buzzy sounds at different scales and intensities.

cross_references.title: Cassin's Finch (Degree 33)
cross_references.connection: DRY WOODLAND CONNECTION. Cassin's Finch = mountain conifer (ponderosa at 1,000-3,000 m, closed canopy, high elevation). Black-throated Gray = oak-pine (ponderosa/Garry oak at 0-1,500 m, open canopy, low elevation). Both dry-side species with ponderosa pine association. The engine's dry woodland belt now spans from lowland oaks to mountain pines.

cross_references.title: Red Crossbill (Degree 34)
cross_references.connection: CONIFER CANOPY FORAGERS. Both forage in tree canopy in dry-side habitats. Crossbill works cones for seeds. Warbler works leaves for insects. Same landscape, different foraging guilds.

cross_references.title: Wings of the Pacific Northwest
cross_references.connection: Black-throated Gray Warbler documented as summer breeding visitor to dry woodland habitats in western Washington and Oregon. Locally common in Garry oak woodlands. One of approximately 25 Parulidae species regularly occurring in the PNW.

cross_references.title: Living Systems of the PNW
cross_references.connection: Garry oak woodland ecology. Fire-dependent ecosystem dynamics. Douglas fir encroachment from fire suppression. Oak savanna restoration. One of the PNW's most endangered ecosystem types. Less than 10% of historical Garry oak extent remains.

engine_state.energy_note: E=4 continues. Now at 15 degrees (38.5%). E=3 absent for 18 DEGREES (since degree 20) -- WORSENING. E=5 at 3 degrees (7.7%). The dynamic range compression deepens: degrees 21-38 have been exclusively E=4 and E=5. NO E=3, E=2, or E=1 in 18 degrees. The lower half of the energy spectrum is completely absent from the recent arc.
engine_state.passeriformes_returns: Passeriformes returns after three consecutive Apodiformes (35-37). The hummingbird trio is closed. The engine returns to songbirds.
engine_state.ecosystem_note: Oak-pine -- FIRST DRY WOODLAND in the engine. All 37 previous degrees used wet, urban, montane, aerial, or generalized habitats. The engine adds a new biome axis: dry woodland. Garry oak ecosystems are among the most endangered in the PNW.
engine_state.acoustic_element_39: THE DRAWL -- a slow, buzzy, unhurried warbler song from an oak-pine canopy. After three hummingbird elements (thrum, sonic boom, filament), the engine decelerates into warbler time. Lazy tempo, buzzy timbre, steady delivery. The most relaxed sound in the Parulidae.
engine_state.parulidae_note: SIXTH PARULIDAE -- now the engine's largest family with 6 members. Returns after 18-degree absence (longest family gap followed by return). Quintet (14-17, 20) was all E=3, wet-side, clean-toned. Sixth member is E=4, dry-side, buzzy. The quintet is closed. A new Parulidae chapter opens.
engine_state.conservation_note: LEAST CONCERN but Garry oak habitat declining. The species is secure across its range but its primary PNW habitat (Garry oak woodland) is critically endangered. Less than 10% of historical extent remains. Fire suppression is the primary driver.
critical_gaps: No marine/shoreline species in 38 degrees of 'The Sound of Puget Sound' -- 38 DEGREES OVERDUE -- EXISTENTIAL CRISIS (WORSENING)
No corvid (Steller's Jay, crow, raven) -- 38 degrees -- URGENT
No nocturnal species since degree 4 (34 degrees ago) -- OVERDUE
E=3 not used since degree 20 (18 degrees ago) -- dynamic range compression WORSENING
No E=2 since degree 13 (25 degrees ago) -- LOW ENERGY ABSENT
No E=1 since degree 12 (26 degrees ago) -- MINIMUM ENERGY ABSENT
No marine species remains the engine's defining structural deficit -- 38 DEGREES OF A PROJECT NAMED FOR PUGET SOUND
